tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary loop is loading 1112 bytes (139 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 788 bytes (98 double precision FP elements).

hint.workaround: Unroll your loop if trip count is significantly higher than target unroll factor and if some data references are common to consecutive iterations. This can be done manually. Or with the unroll (resp. unroll_and_jam) directive on top of the inner (resp. surrounding) loop. You can enforce an unroll factor: #pragma unroll_and_jam N, unroll_and_jam(N), unroll N or unroll(N)

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 138.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 8% of peak load performance is reached (8.06 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 8% of peak store performance is reached (5.71 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

header: 0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 138.00 to 48.83 cycles (2.83x speedup).

gain.workaround: Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.
gain.details: Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is probably not vectorized.
Only 13% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

gain.workaround:
- Read less array elements
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by:
 - reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
 - writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

potential.title: 512-bits vectorization
potential.txt: On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:479,501,1175-1177,1184.
The related source loop is not unrolled or unrolled with no peel/tail loop.
Warnings:
 - Ignoring paths for analysis
 - Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 121 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=121
 - RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
 - Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.
This loop has 121 execution paths.
The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
 - hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
 - turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX

Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = (x<0 ? 0 : x) (or MAX(0,x) after defining the corresponding macro)
